questions.question: State one key advantage and one key disadvantage of a shared-memory system compared to a distributed-memory system. (3 marks)
keyPoints: Advantage: Easier to program due to a global address space and implicit data sharing.
Disadvantage: Limited scalability due to memory/bus bandwidth constraints and cache coherence overhead.

questions.question: Explain the difference between UMA and NUMA memory architectures with examples. (3 marks)
keyPoints: UMA (Uniform Memory Access): All processors have equal access time to all memory locations. Example: SMP (Symmetric Multiprocessing) systems like a dual-socket server.

NUMA (Non-Uniform Memory Access): Access time depends on memory location relative to the processor. Local memory is faster than remote memory. Example: Multi-socket servers with per-socket memory banks.
Both are forms of shared-memory architecture with a single logical address space.

questions.question: Compare and contrast shared-memory and distributed-memory architectures. Cover memory organization, programming model, scalability, and a key hardware challenge for each. (8 marks)
keyPoints: Memory Organization: Shared-memory has a single global address space; Distributed-memory has private per-processor memory.
Programming Model: Shared-memory uses multithreading (e.g., OpenMP); Distributed-memory uses message passing (e.g., MPI).
Scalability: Shared-memory is limited to 10s-100s of cores; Distributed-memory is highly scalable to 1000s+ of cores.
Hardware Challenge: Shared-memory must manage cache coherence; Distributed-memory relies on a high-speed, low-latency interconnect network.
Data Sharing: Implicit in shared-memory via memory reads/writes; Explicit in distributed-memory via send/receive operations.
Cost: Shared-memory systems are more expensive to scale; Distributed-memory uses commodity hardware for cost-effective scaling.

questions.question: Why are modern HPC systems hybrid architectures? Explain how MPI and OpenMP work together in such systems. (5 marks)
keyPoints: Modern HPC systems are clusters of multi-core nodes -- each node is a shared-memory machine, but nodes communicate over a network (distributed memory).
MPI handles inter-node communication using message passing between separate address spaces.
OpenMP handles intra-node parallelism using threads that share memory within a single node.
This hybrid approach leverages OpenMP's efficiency for local data sharing and MPI's scalability for large systems.
It reduces MPI message overhead by having fewer MPI processes (one per node instead of one per core) while still utilizing all cores via OpenMP threads.
